speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two with Sandor Klein. Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, May, I received induction paper from the armed services that said within 24 hours I had to go to the city of Vac, spelled V-A-C. That was 30, 35 miles from Budapest. That's where I was called in. The unit number was 102-228. That was the unit. I had arrived there. We got half uniform. We got an army hat, army jacket, army shirt. And we got our-- a gun. The first two days when they put the group together-- which existed of 120 person-- we were assigned to a army barrack. And for three days, we got the military--

speaker: interviewer
text: Training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Training with the weapon and with all this. After the third year, an order came that we have to give back the weapon. We still could keep the half, upper half of the uniform and the cap, which we had it about for a week, another week. And then they said that you not belong to the armed services with weapon. You will be called helpers to the army, working, working group to the army. That was our official name. And then they divided us different groups. They went by trade. They went by age. And somehow they, they checked the strength of the-- of the physical strength of the body. We were put together in troops. And we were told that we will be shipped out to different places in the county. That was on a Sunday morning. And I told one of my friend that I would like to go home to see my mother before we will be shipped out. When I arrived home, my mother lived in the ghetto. They put all the Jews together. And we were visiting in a house. I cannot even tell you how many people were put in one house. They said it that every Jew is eligible for a certain square meter-- let's say a square yard each-- space in a house. So they were put together like, [SIGHS] like rats together. Some person in the hall didn't even have a bed. They just had the chair. My mother was lucky. She had the chair in the corner. And she was crying. And it was time to go off. This friend of mine whom I went home, his mother was also in the same house. And we said goodbye. And that was the last time I saw my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went back to the group, and we got the first surprise. They said that you cannot go back to this unit because that has already the 120 person. You have to go to another one. And we started to look around. How come we cannot go? We both are in the textile industry. I was a weaver. And my friend was a knitter. And my boss somehow get to this city also from the different places where he was serving. And I saw him in the line. And I asked him, can you do something? He said, no. We are unit already put together. And we are going to be shipped out to the city of Szentendre. That's where was a big, big Hungarian weaving factory, textile factory. And I was not crying, but I was begging the sergeant, will you please put two of us over there? Even my boss is there. Let me go with them. No, no, no, you have to go to another group. So that was the story in Vac. We were-- they went to Szentendre, and I was shipped up with the group to Mosonmagyarovar. That's up close to the Austrian border. And we were put in a group who will help the construction in a small city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told that we would like to go there. Is there any chance to go to the vill-- no. That was the end of it. For Mosonmagyarovar, where we were under the open air-- rain, sunshine, you name it-- we were shipped down to Pecs. The city's name spells P-- P-E-C-S. It's down on the southern border of, of the Hungary. And we were over there for months and months. I cannot recall exactly the date, how long we were over there. We were working first in the construction, helping the people who were doing the labor, mixing the mortar, carrying the bricks, cleaning the ground, cleaning every material which they didn't use. It was not really very bad. We had our three meals a day. Naturally, it was army meal. And couple of months after this labor, I went into the kitchen. Somehow, I got close to the man who was our leader. I don't know what kind of-- he was not a sergeant. He was higher than the sergeant. I don't know how you say, especially in English. And I told him that I can work in the kitchen because I learned how to cook when I was a Boy Scout. So he put me in the kitchen. Naturally, who is working in the kitchen has more to eat than the rest. So it was not really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: After months, we went from Pecs. We were shipped to Szombathely. That's where the last air field, army airfield, was, closer to the Austrian border. Over there, we were put a group of 10, working with the SS soldiers whose job was disposing the un-- how you say it-- the bomb which is not blown up, bomb that they dropped down? So anyway, it was a dud, dud, a dud bomb. Our job was taking out the head from the bomb and the timer. There was a big accident over there. One bomb just blown up. And some people died right over there with the bomb. That was our job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I learned it through the months that if you work for a certain group, they don't pay you, but they keep you alive better than the rest of the group. So we ate the German food. And even, we got friendly with the SS soldier who was our guard, day in, day out. One day, we got-- through the army radio, we got the news that I had to go back to-- this mentioned is that the group which went to Szentendre, the so-called textile group, the factory where they were working was bombed. on. And each and every single one of them died. So that somehow, these bad things turned out lucky for me. From Szombathely, the name was-- the airfield name was Sopte, S-O with the umlaut, P-T-E. That's a small airfield that used only by the German army. That's why they were bombed, day in, day out. I was over there until 1945, February. That was the time we were shipped to Germany-- oh, say in Austria. We crossed the border. The SS and the Hungarian army personnel who helped us to get to the borderline were told to go back to Hungary. And we were taken over by the Austrian Sturmfuhrer. That was an elite group of the German army. We were assigned to a barrack, which was really a barn before the war. And we lived over there. We were put together again, a big group from different directions. And we had Hitlerjugends. They were our guards. They were even younger than we are, and they all carried a machine gun. Funny, it sounds very funny, but sometimes when we went out to work, these Hitlerjugend asked us if we would carry his machine gun because it's too heavy for him to carry. That was the funny part of it. That was the first time in my life where I was restricted to one meal a day. We got the meal at night. In the morning when we went out to work, we all got a piece of German Brot, brown bread. But they-- that was our meal, our food for the whole day. And that night when we came back, we got the usually dinner, a very big soup. And sometimes we got some potato. And I think once a week, we got meat also. We were working on, on the borderline. They said that the Russian army is coming, and we have to dig big, big foxholes for them. So when the tanks will come, the tank will fall into these holes. That will stop-- at least, that's what they thought-- that will stop the, the Russian arm, which is coming down from Hungary. And we worked over there until about a month. That's when we started a march. They didn't tell us where are we going. They put the group together. They exchanged the Hitlerjugend for SS soldiers. And we started the March. First time, first day, second day, third day, it was almost fun, like we went for a, an outing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the February-- it's March. It was close to end of March. It was getting--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had clothing that time what we had left. When we were-- the army uniforms were taken away, we got some civilian clothing, talking about the pants, pair of shoe, shirt. And some people even had a jacket. I had a plaid blanket with me. That's what I used for coat. It was not very strong winter, but it was still cold. And our march started to go to the Austrian Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shoes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a pair of good shoe because that was made by my father, and that's what I took it-- I took with me when I was inducted in the service, because we didn't have pants, army pants and army shoe, we didn't have. So we had our own. And that was a good pair of shoes, thank god. I went on these and marched all the days with these shoes. And we went about two days. We arrived to the Semmering Pass in Austria. This is one of the highest points in the lower Alps over there. That's when our march turned into it a dead march. We usually walked from late afternoon till early morning. Daytime, we had to rest side of the road, wherever we could sit down and whatever we could grab to eat, talking about dandelion, grass, snails. Just picked up the snail from the grass, pulled out the two prong above the eyes, and we ate the snail. That's why if somebody mentioned the name now-- how you call it-- escargot, my stomach start to turn. That was our escargot. We ate everything. You won't believe. We drink the water from the mud. We melt that snow in our hand. That's how we tried to survive. We left the town before the pass around 6:00 at night. It was already dark. I cannot tell you how many people were put together in this march. And we still didn't know where are we going. We arrived to the highest point. And suddenly, we saw a line of SS soldiers with machine gun in their hand. And we were told the famous word, which from there on, I heard almost every day, almost every minute of the day, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- let's go. Let's go. Let's go. Do it faster, faster. Just keep on going. And then we arrived to the point. And suddenly, a guy whom I know even back from Hungary, his head was blown up right into my face. Picture this. I am marching, and suddenly, a part of a head comes to your face. And the German soldier standing outside just said the famous word, [GERMAN]. And we were marching. And we were walking through the dead body, because as the peak started to go downhill, that's where they were shooting with the machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Random, random, just shooting, period. And down from the peak when the sun came up again, we were stopped. And the Germans said, we need 10 people to clean the road. We didn't know what does it mean, clean off the snow or whatever. But I learned through my months that if you work, you got better service.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put my hand up. 10 of us, we had to leave our backpack, whatever we had, leave in that place. And we were marching back on the top of the hill. We had to clear out of the dead bodies. They had some wagons over there-- not cars, a wagon, horse-drawn wagon. We had to put the bodies on this wagon and spread the snow all over to cover the blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we finished the street cleaning, one of the German soldier came to us. And I understood him because I learned German in the school. I didn't speak the language. I just listened, and I observed what they are saying. Now, you can look through the leftover from the dead body. And whatever you feel like, take it. That will be yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took a piece of soap. I remember clearly. I took a hair comb. And I took a jacket. So I figured out if I have a jacket, I won't have to use this wet blanket, which was from the snow, from the rain, from the mud, already wet and heavy. So I took a jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went down back to the group. We got new soldiers. They were very friendly. They were singing. And they even asked us if we knew any song what we can sing in the march. So we marched from there further on. We  went through Neukirchen, which is close to Wiener Neustadt in Austria, like an outskirt of Vienna. There, we had a whole day and a whole night rest. Then we went through the other march. We arrived to the city of Steyr. I don't know if it's the big factory name was Goergin or Goebbels, one of the two. Goering or whatever, that's where we stopped. And they said that we will work in this factory. We were there for two days waiting for assignment. And suddenly, they said, we have to go-- no pack, nothing, just as you are. Then from there, we marched through Enns. And on March, it was March-- April, we arrived to Mauthausen. Mauthausen was, I don't know in order, but one of the famous concentration camp which the Germans put up in Austria. And after the camp, there was-- the camp was full. They had buildings. And they had a so-called Zelt, which is tent, blue and white, huge tent. Some of them had wooden floor. Some of them had no floor at all, just clay and mud, what they have over there. And there was no sanitary facilities, absolutely none. On the side of the barrack, there were a big, deep hole dug that was the so-called latrine, which was the so-called bathroom. And they had an open pipe for water. That's where we had to clean. That's what we had to drink. And that's where we were. The camp, when the daylight came and we looked around, was surrounded with an electrical wire fence and guard towers, I don't know how big distance one from the other. Three days, we were just wandering in the camp, looking for people whom we are-- who we-- find who we know, or we knew before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there came the famous question, who wants to work? I followed my best and I said, I am willing to work. Can you understand German order? I said, if it's not too long and not to difficult, I can. OK, then you are in this group. So that was 10 of us. And we had to march through the camp. That's how I saw the tent with the wooden floor and dirt floor. People were already outside because there were no room inside the tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were marched about 20 minutes. And then there was big commotion at the door. There was no sign what we will do. We went through a door. And inside they said, this is a place where you will work. There will be no talk. You will come every morning, 4:00. You will work until around noon time. Then you will march back to your tent or the place where you're coming. And you don't talk. You don't say a word, or you will be dead. We still didn't know what we are going to do. We all were assigned a big leather apron. That's a full length leather apron. And they opened the door for us. And suddenly, we felt like we are in a steel factory where they making the steel when it's melting. There was hot and stink. And then here comes the hard part. I was assigned to a [NON-ENGLISH] in the gas chamber. My job was putting the dead body with another man-- man, my age-- under steel stretcher, put the stretcher through the oven door, and close the door. And we had to wait about 10, 15 minutes. The door had to be opened. The steel stretcher came out. And the next body from the mound were put on the stretcher, back to the oven. And that kept on going at eight or nine hours every day. At the beginning, no matter how strict people are that you not allowed to talk, still you could get a word from the man who is working about-- six feet from me, there was another oven and another one, and another one. You just looked around. There was nothing but oven and oven and mound and mounds of dead body. We were told that the first 10, that the 10 men who worked over there before us, they are already in the oven. So we started to be scared that will be our time also. But life had to go on. We were pushed, the guard at the door. One guard inside was sitting next to a fan, cooling himself. And we were just doing it one after the other. After the time we left the work, we all had to lift up the big leather apron. And one guy from the back side of the oven shoveled ashes into our apron, which we had to go outside and spread it on the mud. So where the German soldiers had their so-called officers, when they come into the workshop, they didn't have to walk through with the shiny boot through the mud. That was our job. We found out that the group who were working before us, they started on the same day. And they were told that they will work over there 10 days, and they will get an assignment. The eighth day came, and we started to pray. Whatever, whoever could say, we were praying. And that's how we marched to the work. That was the best day of our working days over there because we passed by a wagon which was full of bread. And we stole the bread, even though we know that for a potato peel, you will get shot. But we stole the bread. And we ate. And we went to the work. That helped us going through the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ninth day, we finished the work. And we went home. We were in a mud floor tent. Big commotion in the tent-- what's going on? Rush, rush. [GERMAN]. Didn't know where, what-- they chased out from the camp. That was the fastest three day run I ever did in my life. That was on the end of April-- 20-- 24, 25, and 26th of April. Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were chased out from the camp of Mauthausan. And we heard on the second day that the Russian army already liberated the camp of Mauthausen. We were chased through three days and three nights without food, without stopping. Then we arrived to Gunskirchen. That was a small, unknown camp within a forest. We had to go through a row of drums, which we found out next day that they are full of gasoline.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German army was pressed. The American army was already down close to Salzburg, which was about 70 kilometers from the Gunskirchen camp. The Mauthausen side, the Russian army was coming already. And naturally, again, work-- what will be your work? This camp did not have gas chamber or oven. This one had killing place. Row by row, people were stood up and just machine gunned them down, one by one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our job was carrying these dead bodies into a big, open grave, and put them in one layer of body, one layer of this white stuff, lye, smelled chlorine. That's what we had to cover the body-- layer after layer after layer, no water, no food, no bread, no nothing. We heard nights the guns already. We were in this camp eight days. May 4, 1945 came. In the corner of the barrack where we were, we were having and saying our Friday night prayer-- I don't know, six, seven, eight of us together. We're saying the prayer. And suddenly, we saw the first-- we didn't know who they are. A soldier came in a tank, top of the tank. It's a good time to stop.
